If this field is empty, this operation retrieves the selected objects from the time the Mule app is started.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Primary Node Only</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Determines whether to execute this source only on the primary node when running Mule instances in a cluster.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Scheduling Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><p>scheduling-strategy</p></div></div></td><td><p>Configures the scheduler that triggers the polling.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Redelivery Policy</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#RedeliveryPolicy">Redelivery Policy Type</a></p></div></div></td><td><p>Defines a policy for processing the redelivery of the same message.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>reconnect</p></li><li><p>reconnect-forever</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>A retry strategy in case of connectivity errors.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Output

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Attributes Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Any</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### For Configurations

- config

## On New Objects

`<ldap:new-objects>`

Triggers when a new LDAP entry is added.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Configuration</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The name of the configuration to use.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Base DN</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>The base DN of the LDAP search.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Filter</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>A valid LDAP filter. The LDAP connector supports LDAP search filters as defined in RFC 2254.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Attributes</p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><p>A list of the attributes to return in the result. If the attributes list is empty or null, then by default all LDAP entry attributes are returned.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Scope</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>OBJECT</p></li><li><p>ONE_LEVEL</p></li><li><p>SUB_TREE</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>The scope of the search. Valid attributes are:</p><p>* OBJECT: Searches only the entry at the base DN so that only results from that entry are returned. This must meet the search filter criteria. * ONE_LEVEL: Searches all entries one level under the base DN and does not include entries in the base DN or any entries one level under the base DN. * SUB_TREE: Searches all entries at all levels under and including the specified base DN.</p></td><td><p>ONE_LEVEL</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Structural Object Class</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>The type of entry to return. This is used for DataSense in Anypoint Studio IDE and has no impact on Mule.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Since</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Specify a date in <code>YYYYMMDDhhmmssâZ'</code> format, which is the way LDAP formats dates (for example, <code>20170317163040Z</code>). If this field is empty, this operation retrieves the selected objects from the time the Mule app is started.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Primary Node Only</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Determines whether to execute this source only on the primary node when running Mule instances in a cluster.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Scheduling Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><p>scheduling-strategy</p></div></div></td><td><p>Configures the scheduler that triggers the polling.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Redelivery Policy</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#RedeliveryPolicy">Redelivery Policy Type</a></p></div></div></td><td><p>Defines a policy for processing the redelivery of the same message.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>reconnect</p></li><li><p>reconnect-forever</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>A retry strategy in case of connectivity errors.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Output

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Attributes Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Any</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### For Configurations

- config

## Reconnection

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Fails Deployment</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>When the application is deployed, a connectivity test is performed on all connectors. If set to true, deployment fails if the test doesnât pass after exhausting the associated reconnection strategy.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>reconnect</p></li><li><p>reconnect-forever</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>The reconnection strategy to use.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Reconnect

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Frequency</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>How often in milliseconds to reconnect.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Count</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>How many reconnection attempts to make.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>blocking</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><p>If false, the reconnection strategy runs in a separate, non-blocking thread.</p></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Reconnect Forever

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Frequency</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>How often in milliseconds to reconnect.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>blocking</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><p>If false, the reconnection strategy runs in a separate, non-blocking thread.</p></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Expiration Policy Type

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Max Idle Time</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>A scalar time value for the maximum amount of time a dynamic configuration instance should be allowed to be idle before itâs considered eligible for expiration.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Time Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>A time unit that qualifies the maxIdleTime attribute.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Repeatable In-Memory Iterable Type

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Initial Buffer Size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>The amount of instances to initially allow to be kept in memory to consume the stream and provide random access to it. If the stream contains more data than can fit into this buffer, the buffer expands according to the bufferSizeIncrement attribute, with an upper limit of maxInMemorySize. Default value is 100 instances.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Buffer Size Increment</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>This is by how much the buffer size expands if it exceeds its initial size. Setting a value of zero or lower means that the buffer should not expand, and to raise a STREAM_MAXIMUM_SIZE_EXCEEDED error when the buffer gets full. Default value is 100 instances.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Max Buffer Size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>The maximum amount of memory to use. If more than that is used, then a STREAM_MAXIMUM_SIZE_EXCEEDED error is raised. A value lower than or equal to zero means no limit.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Repeatable File Store Iterable Type

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Max In Memory Size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>The maximum amount of instances to keep in memory. If more than that is required, then it starts to buffer the content on disk.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Buffer Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>The unit in which maxInMemorySize is expressed.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## TLS Type

Configures TLS to provide secure communications for the Mule app.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Enabled Protocols</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>A comma-separated list of protocols enabled for this context.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enabled Cipher Suites</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>A comma-separated list of cipher suites enabled for this context.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Trust Store</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#TrustStore">Truststore Type</a></p></div></div></td><td><p>Configures the TLS truststore.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Key Store</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#KeyStore">Keystore Type</a></p></div></div></td><td><p>Configures the TLS keystore.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Revocation Check</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#standard-revocation-check">Standard Revocation Check Type</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#custom-ocsp-responder">Custom OCSP Responder Type</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#crl-file">CRL File Type</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Configures certificate revocation checking.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Truststore Type

Configures the truststore for TLS.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Path</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Path to the truststore. Mule resolves the path relative to the current classpath and file system.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Password</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Password used to protect the truststore.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Type of store.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Algorithm</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Encryption algorithm that the truststore uses.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Insecure</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>If <code>true</code>, Mule stops performing certificate validations. Setting this to <code>true</code> can make connections vulnerable to attacks.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Keystore Type

Configures the keystore for the TLS protocol. The keystore you generate contains a private key and a
public certificate.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Path</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Path to the keystore. Mule resolves the path relative to the current classpath and file system.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Type of store.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Alias</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Alias of the key to use when the keystore contains multiple private keys. By default, Mule uses the first key in the file.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Key Password</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Password used to protect the private key.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Password</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Password used to protect the keystore.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Algorithm</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Encryption algorithm that the keystore uses.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Standard Revocation Check Type

Configures standard revocation checks for TLS certificates.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Only End Entities</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Which elements to verify in the certificate chain:</p><p>* <code>true</code> - Verify only the last element in the certificate chain. * <code>false</code> - Verify all elements in the certificate chain.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Prefer Crls</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>How to check certificate validity:</p><p>* <code>true</code> - Check the Certification Revocation List (CRL) for certificate validity. * <code>false</code> - Use the Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) to check certificate validity.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>No Fallback</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Whether to use the secondary method to check certificate validity:</p><p>* <code>true</code> - Use the method that wasnât specified in the <strong>Prefer Crls</strong> field (the secondary method) to check certificate validity. * <code>false</code> - Do not use the secondary method to check certificate validity.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Soft Fail</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>What to do if the revocation server canât be reached or is busy:</p><p>* <code>true</code> - Avoid verification failure. * <code>false</code> - Allow the verification to fail.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Custom OCSP Responder Type

Configures a custom OCSP responder for certification revocation checks.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Url</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>URL of the OCSP responder.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Cert Alias</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Alias of the signing certificate for the OCSP response. If specified, the alias must be in the truststore.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## CRL File Type

Specifies the location of the certification revocation list (CRL) file.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Path</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Path to the CRL file.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Redelivery Policy Type

Configures the redelivery policy for executing requests that generate errors. You can add a
redelivery policy to any source in a flow.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Max Redelivery Count</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>The maximum number of times that a redelivered request can be processed unsuccessfully before returning a REDELIVERY_EXHAUSTED error.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Use Secure Hash</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>If <code>true</code>, Mule uses a secure hash algorithm to identify a redelivered message.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message Digest Algorithm</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Secure hashing algorithm to use if the <strong>Use Secure Hash</strong> field is <code>true</code>. If the payload of the message is a Java object, Mule ignores this value and returns the value that the payloadâs <code>hashCode()</code> returned.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Id Expression</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>One or more expressions that determine when a message was redelivered. You can set this property only if the <strong>Use Secure Hash</strong> field is <code>false</code>.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Object Store</p></td><td><div><div><p>Object Store</p></div></div></td><td><p>Configures the object store that stores the redelivery counter for each message.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Using Anypoint Studio to Configure a Connector](../../connectors/introduction/intro-config-use-studio)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
---

# Using Studio to Configure LDAP Connector 3.7

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio (Studio) editors help you design and update your Mule applications, properties, and
configuration files.

To add and configure a connector in Studio:

1.  [Create a Mule project](#create-mule-project).
2.  [Add the connector to your Mule project](#add-connector-to-project).
3.  [Configure a source for the connectorâs flow](#configure-source).
4.  [Add a connector operation to the flow](#add-connector-operation).
5.  [Configure a global element for the connector](#configure-global-element).
6.  [Configure the other connector fields](#configure-other-fields).

When you run the connector, you can view the app log to check for problems in real time, as
described in [View the App Log](#view-app-log).

If you are new to configuring connectors in Studio, see
[Using Anypoint Studio to Configure a Connector](../../connectors/introduction/intro-config-use-studio).
If, after reading this topic, you need additional information about the connector fields, see the
[LDAP Connector Reference](ldap-connector-reference).

## Create a Mule Project

In Studio, create a new Mule project in which to add and configure the connector:

1.  In Studio, select **File > New > Mule Project**.
2.  Enter a name for your Mule project and click **Finish**.

## Add the Connector to Your Mule Project

Add Anypoint Connector for Lightweight Directory Access Protocol Connector (LDAP Connector) to your
Mule project to automatically populate the XML code with the connectorâs namespace and schema
location and add the required dependencies to the projectâs `pom.xml` file:

1.  In the **Mule Palette** view, click **(X) Search in Exchange**.
2.  In **Add Dependencies to Project**, type `ldap` in the search field.
3.  Click **LDAP Connector** in **Available modules**.
4.  Click **Add**.
5.  Click **Finish**.

Adding a connector to a Mule project in Studio does not make that connector available to other
projects in your Studio workspace.

## Configure a Source

A source initiates a flow when a specified condition is met. You can configure one of these sources
to use with LDAP Connector:

- On Modified Objects

  Initiates a flow when an LDAP data object is modified

- On New Objects

  Initiates a flow when a new LDAP data object is created

- HTTP Listener

  Initiates a flow each time it receives a request on the configured host and port

- Scheduler

  Initiates a flow when a time-based condition is met

For example, to configure an **On Modified Objects** source, follow these steps:

1.  In the **Mule Palette** view, select **On Modified objects**.
2.  Drag **On Modified Objects** to the Studio canvas.
3.  If you want to customize the display name, change the value of the **Display Name** field.
4.  In the **Connector configuration** field, either select an existing global element or click the
    plus sign (**+**) to configure a new global element.
5.  On the **General** tab, specify the principal distinguished name (DN) for the connection, the
    authentication mechanism, and the configuration URL.
6.  On the **Advanced** tab, specify reconnection information, including a reconnection strategy.
7.  Click **Test Connection** to confirm that Mule can connect with the specified server.
8.  In the **On Modified Objects** configuration screen, specify a value for the **Base DN** field.
9.  Click **OK**.

## Add a Connector Operation to the Flow

When you add a connector operation to your flow, you are specifying an action for that connector to
perform.

To add an operation for LDAP Connector, follow these steps:

1.  In the **Mule Palette** view, select **LDAP** and then select the desired operation.
2.  Drag the operation onto the Studio canvas, next to the input source.

## Configure a Global Element for the Connector

When you configure a connector, configure a global element that all instances of that connector in
the app can use. Configuring a global element requires you to provide the authentication credentials
that the connector requires to access the target LDAP system.

To configure the global element for LDAP Connector, follow these steps:

1.  Select the operation in the Studio canvas.
2.  In the **General** configuration screen for the operation, click **+** to access the global
    element configuration fields.
3.  In the **General** tab, in **Connection**, select the connection type and then complete the
    associated connection fields.

    For information about configuring the SSL (Deprecated), Secure SSL, and TLS connection types,
    see [Configure an SSL Connection Type (Deprecated)](#ssl-connection-type),
    [Configure a Secure SSL Connection Type](#secure-ssl-connection-type), and
    [Configure a TLS Connection Type](#tls-connection-type).

    You can reference a configuration file that contains ANT-style property placeholders
    (recommended), or you can enter your authorization credentials in the global configuration
    properties. For information about the benefits of using property placeholders and how to
    configure them, see
    [Anypoint Connector Configuration](../../connectors/introduction/intro-connector-configuration-overview).

4.  In the **Advanced** tab, optionally specify reconnection information, including a reconnection
    strategy.
5.  Click **Test Connection** to confirm that Mule can connect with the specified server.
6.  Click **OK**.

### Configure an SSL Connection Type (Deprecated)

> [!IMPORTANT] The SSL connection type is deprecated. Use the
> [Secure SSL connection type](#secure-ssl-connection-type) instead, which accepts a Mule TLS
> context factory and supports FIPS 140-3 compliant deployments.

To configure an SSL connection type, use Anypoint Studio to set up an SSL connection in the LDAP
global element:

1.  In the **Connection** field of the global element, select `SSL Configuration`.
2.  Complete the required fields.

Connect to SSL using port 636.

When you set up an SSL connection type, you can set up one custom truststore. To support this
scenario, the connector overrides the JVM default truststore path, which can affect other processes
that depend on that truststore.

### Configure a Secure SSL Connection Type

The Secure SSL connection type uses a Mule TLS context factory for trust store configuration. Use
this connection type for FIPS 140-3 compliant deployments. It is also supported in non-FIPS
environments with any trust store type provided via **TLS Context** field.

To configure a Secure SSL connection type, use Studio to set up a Secure SSL connection in the LDAP
global element:

1.  In the **Connection** field of the global element, select `Secure SSL Configuration`.
2.  In the **TLS Context** field, click the plus sign (**+**) to configure a new TLS context, or
    select an existing one.
3.  In the TLS context, configure your truststore settings, including the truststore path, password,
    and type.
4.  Complete the remaining required fields, including **Principal DN** and **URL**.
5.  Click **Test Connection** to confirm that Mule can connect with the specified server.
6.  Click **OK**.

Connect to Secure SSL using port 636.

### Configure a TLS Connection Type

To configure a TLS connection type, use Studio to set up a TLS connection in the LDAP global
element:

1.  In the **Connection** field of the global element, select `TLS Configuration`.
2.  Select the **Advanced** Tab.
3.  In the **Extended configurations** field, select **Edit inline** and then add the
    `org.mule.module.ldap.trustStorePath` and `org.mule.module.ldap.trustStorePassword` key values.
4.  Ensure that the native LDAP pooling functionality is turned off. For example if using JNDI, do
    not use attributes such as `com.sun.jndi.ldap.connect.pool=true`, which causes problems when
    using TLS.

Connect to TLS using port 389, using `ldap` instead of `ldaps` in the associated URL.

For an example of configuring a TLS connection, see
[Set Up a Common Truststore](ldap-connector-examples#set-up-common-truststore).

## Configure Additional Connector Fields

After you configure a global element for LDAP Connector, configure the other required fields for the
connector. The required fields vary depending on which connector operation you use.

## View the App Log

To check for problems, you can view the app log as follows:

- If youâre running the app from Anypoint Platform, the app log output goes to the Anypoint Studio
  console window.
- If youâre running the app using Mule from the command line, the app log output goes to your
  operating system console.

Unless the log file path is customized in the appâs log file (`log4j2.xml`), you can also access the
app log in the default location `MULE_HOME/logs/<app-name>.log`. You can configure the location of
the log path in the app log file `log4j2.xml`.

## Next Step

After configuring Studio, you can try using the [LDAP Connector Examples](ldap-connector-examples).

## See Also

- [Using Anypoint Studio to Configure a Connector](../../connectors/introduction/intro-config-use-studio)
- [LDAP Connector Reference](ldap-connector-reference)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
---

# LDAP Connector 3.7 - XML and Maven Support

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Although you can manually code a Mule app in XML, it is more efficient to use Anypoint Studio:

- If you manually code a Mule runtime engine (Mule) app in XML (either from the Anypoint Studio XML
  editor or from a text editor), you can access the connector from your app by adding reference
  statements to both your XML Mule flow and the Apache Maven `pom.xml` file.
- If you add the connector in Studio, Studio automatically populates the XML code with the
  connectorâs namespace and schema location and adds a dependency to the `pom.xml` file.

## Add a Namespace for the Connector

Paste the following code inside the `mule` tag of the header of your configuration XML:

```xml
http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/ldap
http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/ldap/current/mule-ldap.xsd
```

This example shows how the namespace statements are placed in the XML:

```xml
<mule xmlns:ldap="http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/ldap" xmlns="http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/core"
	xmlns:doc="http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/documentation"
	xmlns:spring="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans"
	xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans/spring-beans-current.xsd
http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/core http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/core/current/mule.xsd
http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/ldap http://www.mulesoft.org/schema/mule/ldap/current/mule-ldap.xsd">
```

## Add a POM File Dependency

The Apache Maven `pom.xml` file generated by Anypoint Studio contains dependencies for a Mule app.
When you code a Mule app manually, include this XML snippet in your `pom.xml` file to enable access
to this connector:

```xml
<dependency>
  <groupId>com.mulesoft.connectors</groupId>
  <artifactId>mule-ldap-connector</artifactId>
  <version>x.x.x</version>
  <classifier>mule-plugin</classifier>
</dependency>
```

Replace `<version>` with the version that corresponds to the connector you are using.

To obtain the most up-to-date `pom.xml` file information:

1.  Go to [Anypoint Exchange](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/).
2.  In Exchange, click **Login** and supply your Anypoint Platform username and password.
3.  In Exchange, search for `ldap`.
4.  Select the connector.
5.  Click **Dependency Snippets** near the upper right of the screen.

## Next Step

After completing your namespace and `pom.xml` file, you can try the
[Examples](ldap-connector-examples).

## See Also

- [Introduction to Anypoint Connectors](../../connectors/introduction/introduction-to-anypoint-connectors)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
